text: The non-classical anodic production of H2 from 5-hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) into hydrogen fuel with simultaneous production of valuable chemicals represents a highly desirable route in terms of energy efficiency. Due to the extremely low working potential (~0.1 V vs. RHE), the mechanisms of such reactions remain poorly understood due to the lack of information about intermediates, which are often present only transiently in solution. In this work, the mechanisms of catalytic and non-catalytic production of H2 from HMF are studied using a suite of complementary experimental techniques including in situ Raman and infrared spectroscopy as well as isotope exchange experiments. DFT simulations are also employed. Our experiments show the presence of two gem-diol forms of HMF in solution in alkaline solution, identified as the 5-(dihydroxymethyl)furan-2-methanol anion (DHMFM⁻) and its corresponding dianion (DHMFM²⁻). DHMFM²⁻ is readily oxidized in solution with the formation of hydrogen gas and the overall process proceeds through hydride transfer, while H2 evolution from DHMFM⁻ proceeds with proton transfer and formation of water. Increasing the working potential at the electrode surface results in an acceleration of the oxidation of DHMFM⁻ and corresponding shift of the DHMFM⁻ ↔ DHMFM²⁻ equilibrium toward the neutral species, inhibiting the non-classical production of hydrogen. Thus, by directly observing the key DHMFM²⁻ intermediate of the HMF-aldehyde driven hydrogen evolution process, our studies provide direct insights into the requirements for aldehyde-based hydrogen production and that they provide a foundation for the design of efficient hydrogen production catalysts.
